retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital optic neuropathies : Isolated conditions include dominant and recessive optic atrophy, Leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy, and Behr’s hereditary optic atrophy. Optic atrophy associated with systemic disease or neurological conditions

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Optic atrophy is not a disease per se; it is a sign indicating several conditions. Therefore, the complications are related to the underlying disease, rather than the optic atrophy itself. The direct complication of optic atrophy is vision loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Optic atrophy is a pathological term referring to optic nerve shrinkage caused by the degeneration of retinal ganglion cell (RGC) axons. The term "optic atrophy" is a misnomer since atrophy implies disuse. Therefore, a better term for optic atrophy is "optic neuropathy." However, this term is also controversial since, in certain situations (eg, primary optic atrophy or traumatic brain injury), optic neuropathy may not occur. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Swollen optic nerve: Papilledema, AION

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Recognition of optic atrophy might prove to be life-saving for the patient. Therefore, it is imperative to have adequate knowledge regarding this fairly common condition. This review presents the basic concepts of optic atrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- History and Physical -- Ascending optic atrophy
retrieved_docs.content: Also known as Wallerian degeneration, this anterograde degeneration occurs as a consequence of injury to the retinal elements or optic nerve head axons. The subsequent degeneration ascends towards the LGB and superior colliculus. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The visual prognosis in optic atrophy depends upon the extent of axonal loss. In partial optic atrophy, the visual impairment would be mild to moderate. There may even be no decrease in measured visual acuity but only a decrease in visual field. However, in total optic atrophy, the prognosis is poor. Early and intensive treatment in conditions such as optic neuritis, and toxic or nutritional optic neuropathy, may lead to nearly complete recovery.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Optic atrophy can be classified using different parameters. These parameters can be clinical, pathological, and based upon the extent and etiology. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Retinal disease: Retinitis pigmentosa, macular dystrophies

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In the case of optic atrophy, a number of investigations can be tailored according to the suspected etiology of the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of optic atrophy varies widely. Optic atrophy was one of the five main causes of blindness in prevalence studies from Israel, Japan, Scotland, Zaire, and other countries. [5] [6] [7] [8] [9] . In the Oman eye study, 5% of the blindness was attributable to this condition. [10] In a study from Egypt, the age-adjusted blindness prevalence rates per 1000 persons showed that in an urban population, 4.1% of the blindness and in a rural population 1.2% of the blindness was attributable to optic atrophy. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the varied etiologies linked to optic atrophy, encompassing conditions such as glaucoma, ischemic optic neuropathy, demyelinating disorders, hereditary optic neuropathies, compressive lesions, and toxic or nutritional deficiencies. Differentiate between optic atrophy and other optic nerve–related conditions, distinguishing it from conditions like optic neuritis, papilledema, retinal diseases, and nonorganic visual loss through careful clinical assessment and ancillary testing. Implement comprehensive treatment strategies addressing the specific causative factors, utilizing pharmacological therapies, neuroprotective agents, antiinflammatory medications, or surgical interventions when indicated. Implement prompt and appropriate referrals to specialized ophthalmologists, neurologists, or multidisciplinary teams based on the suspected underlying etiology of optic atrophy or associated systemic conditions. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The risk factors for the development of optic atrophy have been denoted by the mnemonic: VIN DITTCH MD. This mnemonic denotes the following conditions: V: Vascular I: Inflammatory and infectious N: Neoplastic or compressive D: Primary demyelinating disease or idiopathic optic neuritis T: Toxic T: Traumatic C: Congenital H: Hereditary M: Metabolic and endocrine causes D: Degenerative [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Since the cause of optic atrophy is often systemic, relevant consultations should be done. When required, multidisciplinary assessments such as neurology, neurosurgery, and infectious disease can be performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- History and Physical -- Primary optic atrophy
retrieved_docs.content: Primary optic atrophy occurs without any preceding swelling of the optic nerve head. The condition is caused by lesions in the anterior visual system extending from the RGCs to the lateral geniculate body (LGB). The etiology of primary optic atrophy varies from conditions such as compressive, hereditary, traumatic, toxic, nutritional, and inflammatory optic neuropathies. In this condition, the axons degenerate in an orderly manner. Subsequently, the resolution is characterized by the laying down of columns of glial cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: A retrospective case series of nonglaucomatous optic atrophy in Malaysia found the main etiologies to be space-occupying intracranial lesions (26%), congenital or hereditary diseases (13%), hydrocephalus (12%), trauma (12%) and vascular causes (12%). Various conditions in which optic atrophy may occur include:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: (isolated choroidal inflammation), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (combined choroidal‑retinal inflammation), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد (focal unspecified combined inflammation), and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary choroidal atrophy). Collectively, these conditions demonstrate the complex interplay of vascular occlusion, hemorrhage, inflammation, metabolic injury, and degenerative remodeling within the retina and choroid; recognizing each as a child of “Disorders of choroid and retina (H30‑H36)” aids clinicians in systematic diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of targeted therapeutic strategies to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: حصوي (calcific degeneration of choroidal or retinal tissue), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal atrophy) and حثل المشيمية حول الحُليلة، جزئي (partial sclerosis of the choroid surrounding the macula). Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: taxonomy aids clinicians in recognizing the diverse presentations of choroidal and retinal pathology, facilitating accurate diagnosis, targeted management, and coordinated patient care across a range of ocular disorders.

Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2169
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are all described as having a clinical course characterized by sudden onset. The “sudden onset” qualifier indicates that the manifestations of these conditions develop rapidly, which is a key feature for clinicians to recognize when diagnosing and managing these disorders. Understanding that a rapid, abrupt presentation is common to these entities helps guide timely evaluation and appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244

Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

Additional specific sub‑conditions listed under the same category are انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي, تنكس حصوي, and حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي. Together, these entities illustrate the extensive and heterogeneous nature of choroidal and retinal disorders, underscoring the importance of precise classification for effective patient care, targeted research, and accurate medical coding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus—all localized to the retina. The retina is also the primary site for retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various other retinopathies. Therapeutic interventions targeting these lesions comprise destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye, categorized under “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid,” with radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal lesions (e.g., macular edema or tumors) as a further sub‑category. The disease داء ريفسام is likewise identified with retinal findings, underscoring the eye’s role in its manifestation. Clinical assessment employs the concept of “general clinical state” to interpret both biologic function failure and uncontrolled disease conditions; uncontrolled disease conditions are associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the need for vigilant monitoring. Biologic function failure is linked to hepatic‑renal failure, indicating a critical organ‑system

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 181
retrieved_docs.content: conditions of the choroidea comprise secondary choroidal atrophy (انتشار الضمور الثانوي بالمشيمية), primary choroidal degeneration (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), age‑related choroidal atrophy (الضمور الشيخوخي بالمشيمية), calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), partial and total peripapillary choroidal sclerosis (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة, جزئي/كلي), hereditary choroidal sclerosis (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), partial generalized choroidal dystrophy, familial choroidal atrophy (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), and acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE – اعتلال الظهارة الصباغي اللوحي متعدد البؤر الخلفي الحاد). Structural complications include serous choroidal detachment (انفصال المشيمية المصلية), retinal‑choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), hemorrhagic retinal‑choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), dense choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق), and choroidal rupture (التمزق المشيمي). Inflammatory and infectious processes predominantly affect

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2602
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disorder of hemodialysis, First use syndrome of dialysis, Sudden acquired retinal degeneration, and متلازمة اختلال غسيل الكلى (dialysis dysfunction syndrome) are medical conditions that share a common clinical characteristic: a sudden‑onset course. In each of these disorders the disease manifests abruptly, indicating that their clinical course is defined by a rapid appearance of symptoms. Recognizing this sudden‑onset pattern is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of complications related to hemodialysis, initial dialysis use, retinal degeneration, or dialysis dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 610
retrieved_docs.content: (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), is recognized as a distinct manifestation within this classification. Together, these entities illustrate diverse etiologies—vascular, inflammatory, traumatic, postoperative, degenerative, and genetic—that affect the choroid and retina, underscoring the importance of precise classification for diagnosis, management, and prognostication in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية). Both conditions represent severe microvascular complications of long‑standing diabetes mellitus, and the presence of diabetic retinopathy often signals an increased risk of progression to advanced kidney failure. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians, as early detection and management of retinal changes can prompt more aggressive monitoring of renal function, and coordinated care can help mitigate the morbidity associated with simultaneous vision loss and the need for renal replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: and choroid. This hierarchical relationship clarifies the scope of ablative retinal therapies. Additionally, the disease referred to in Arabic as “داء ريفسام” manifests its clinical findings in the retina, reinforcing that retinal involvement—whether from systemic metabolic disorders, primary retinal diseases, or targeted retinal lesion destruction—requires precise identification of the retinal site for accurate diagnosis and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 236
retrieved_docs.content: (ندبة مشيمية شبكية). Hemorrhagic choroidal manifestations are noted as choroidal hemorrhage and massive choroidal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي باثق). Perivascular deposits (بُقَعُ روث) are also recorded. Collectively, these entities illustrate the intersection of systemic vascular disease with specific retinal and choroidal hemorrhagic findings, neovascular disorders, inflammatory and infectious processes, traumatic and postoperative sequelae, and degenerative changes, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and prognostic assessment in ophthalmic care.
